results.answer:
Based on the provided context, the answer depends on **which GI Bill chapter** the veteran is using:

## Chapter 33 (Post-9/11 GI Bill)
Avocational or recreational courses are **NOT prohibited**. As noted in Chunk 1:

> *"Courses that are considered avocational or recreational in nature are **not** prohibited, even though they are precluded under chapters 30, 32, and 35 of title 38, U.S.C., and chapter [1606] of title 10, U.S.C."* [1]

## Chapters 30, 32, 35 (title 38) and Chapter 1606 (title 10)
Avocational or recreational courses are **precluded** under these programs. [1]

However, certain courses that are *presumed* avocational or recreational **may still be approved** if the veteran can provide evidence that the course will be of **bona fide use in the pursuit of a business or occupation**. Such evidence can include:
- A statement on the application explaining the business or occupational objective
- A statement from someone in the business showing the need and use of the course [2]

Courses presumed avocational include photography, entertainment, music, dancing, sports, athletics, and similar subjects. [2]

**In summary:** Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) veterans have the broadest access to avocational courses, while veterans under other chapters face restrictions unless they can demonstrate occupational relevance.

results.query: Can VA pay for a private pilot's certificate course at a flight school?
results.expected_answer: No. Vocational flight training for a private pilot's certificate is always prohibited. However, it may be taken at an IHL if it is for credit toward a college degree.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 8 - Periodic Education Certifications
heading_path: 8.05  VA FORM 22-6553c, MONTHLY CERTIFICATION OF FLIGHT TRAINING
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  The student and flight school complete VA Form 22-6553c, Monthly Certification of Flight Training, each month to show the number of hours of flight instruction completed.  VA requires the form to release payment to a student pursuing flight training.

(2)  If [the RPO receives a flight certification and does not have an award, that activity] will prepare an Optional Form 41, Routing and Transmittal Slip, noting the lack of an award, and forward it with the flight certification to the RPO Education Division.  If an enrollment certificate is not in the   claimant's folder, the Education Division will prepare a dictated letter to the student with a copy to the school.  Explain that VA may not pay for training until it receives a properly completed enrollment certification.  Include a statement of procedural and appellate rights with the letter.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 1 - Enrollment Certification
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER II. SPECIFIC ENTRIES ON ENROLLMENT CERTIFICATION
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. If the claimant is pursuing a vocational flight program, the training establishment must complete the following items on Side B of VA Form 22-1999 in addition to the student identification items:

1) Type of Training

2) Name of Program

3) Credit for Previous Training

4) Credit Allowed for Previous Training and Certificates and Ratings. Flight schools and training centers may show credit granted as hours of instruction or ratings held by the student.

EXAMPLE: The student with a commercial certificate and a flight instructor rating enrolls in a multiengine course. The school or training center may show credit granted as "Commercial and Flight Instructor."

(A) A flight school and flight training center must grant credit for previous training and shorten a flight course for VA purposes. If a student transfers from one flight school or flight training center to another, the receiving school or training center determines the credit to grant.

(B) VCEs should review the student's application, military record, and other VA training to determine what aviation experience or flight training the student had. As appropriate, the VCE may need to develop for credit for prior training when the school or training center does not grant credit or grants inadequate credit. If development is necessary, refer to Part IV, Chapter 4.

(C) FAA Requirements. Under FAR 141.77, full course credit may transfer from one certified flight school to another certified flight school. The receiving school must give the student a flight check or written test. The school may credit a student with 50 percent or more of the course requirements based on previous pilot experience and knowledge.

(D) Medical Certificate. Flight schools and training centers certify on VA Form 22-1999, Enrollment Certification, that the student has a private pilot's certificate and that they have copies of the required medical certificate. For the initial enrollment certification for a flight course, flight schools should put the name and date of the medical certificate in the Remarks section of the enrollment certification. If this medical certificate information is not submitted, VCEs will develop for it. If the medical certificate is not valid on the day that the course begins, VCEs will deny the claim. (Refer to Part 4, Chapter 2 for additional processing issues regarding flight training).

NOTE: If the Enrollment Certification is accompanied by VA Form 22-6553c, Monthly Certification of Flight Training, the VCE should accept the certification shown on this form as to the student's medical certificate.

5) Date Training Began in Current Course. This should be certified as the first date the claimant attended training or the date the student agrees for school to certify, whichever is later.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
heading_path: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
retrieved_chunks.content:
2. If a student with a commercial pilot certificate (glider) requests a flight instructor (glider) course, VCEs should approve the request without further development.  Veterans Affairs (VA) presumes the purpose in taking the course is vocational.

3. The commercial pilot certification course is the only free balloon course the State Approving Agency (SAA) may approve. The student may show a final objective of either commercial pilot or flight instructor. The holder of a commercial pilot certificate (free balloon) may teach free ballooning.

d. Add-On Courses.

1. If the student enrolls in a regular commercial pilot certificate course (single or multi-engine land), the school must grant appropriate credit and shorten the course accordingly.  Even if the flight school or flight training center grants credit, this method usually requires more hours and is more expensive than enrolling in an add-on course for the rating.

2. A flight school or flight training center offering an approved add-on course for an aircraft rating should not enroll a student in the regular commercial pilot course. The only exception is if the regular course is less expensive after granting credit for previous training.

e.  For flight training at an Institution of Higher Learning (IHL) the student must follow the program outline and the pre-requisites listed.  If the program is fully contracted with a vocational flight program, then the student is held to the pattern listed above in the general method of pursuit.  If the IHL is operating under 14 CFR Part 61 then the IHL must adhere to the general course progression as listed in Part 61.

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
heading_path: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
2.10  COMBINATION COLLEGE DEGREE – FLIGHT TRAINING PROGRAMS
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. College Degrees.

1. All students must have a  second-class medical certificate prior to the approval and start of any flight training program, unless the training is conducted under FAR Part 61.  Students do not have to take flight courses in the order specified in subparagraph 2.03a (2).

2. A student may receive benefits for private pilot certification provided the course is listed under 14 CFR Part 141 as an approved part of the standard college degree the student is pursing, or is listed as in house 14 CFR Part 61 training and approved by the State Approving Agency (SAA).

3. Flight schools and flight training centers, under contract with colleges or universities, may only provide flight training for all programs above private pilot and must be identified on their Part 141 Air Agency Certificate

4. The degree requirements must specifically identify the required number flight hours for each flight course in the school catalog. Training beyond the degree requirements is vocational.  Students under chapters 30, 32, 33 and 1606, may interrupt their college program and receive reimbursement for flight training above the private pilot level at a flight school or flight training center.  The student may return to college training without being charged with a change of program if they meet all of the following conditions:

[i.] The flight training pursued at the flight school or flight training center does not exceed the amount required for the degree.

[ii.] The college or university grants full credit for the flight course(s) in partial fulfillment of the degree requirements.

[iii.] Students continue to pursue the same degree programs.

b. Chapter 35.

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: M22-4, Part 10, Compliance Survey. Chapter 04, Procedures Applicable to Facilities Offering Specific Types of Training.
heading_path: M22-4, Part 10, Compliance Survey. Chapter 04, Procedures Applicable to Facilities Offering Specific Types of Training.
SUBCHAPTER 03. AREAS OF REVIEW FOR FLIGHT SCHOOLS
4.29 CREDIT FOR PREVIOUS EDUCATION AND TRAINING
retrieved_chunks.content:
3.  Course credits may not be transferred from a non-certificated school to a certificated school.

4. Schools holding examining authority under FAR 141, Subpart D, may recommend graduates of its approved certification courses for pilot certificates and ratings except for flight instructor certificates, Airline Transport Pilot (ATP) certificates and ratings, and turbojet type ratings, without the student taking the FAA flight or written test, or both.  However, the student must have graduated from the course of training offered at the school. Schools with examining authority must consider and grant appropriate credit for previous experience and training of VA students. Therefore, students when enrolled in schools with examining authority must take the required FAA written or flight test, or both, as appropriate. This means a student can take part of a course at one FAR 141 certificated school, transfer to another FAR 141 certificated school, graduate from the course, and have the second school recommend him/her for a pilot certificate or rating.

5. If a VA student has passed the FAA written examination for a rating or certificate before enrolling in a flight course, the student must be credited with no less than 50 percent of the required ground school hours for the course. If ground school training in preparation for the test was received at a certificated school, full credit would normally be given. However, under FAR 141.77, the student must still complete all the curriculum requirements of the course in order to be issued a graduation certificate. Therefore, even though an eligible student has passed the FAA written examination, assistance payments may still be afforded for that ground school instruction which is necessary to complete course requirements.

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, VA payment for a private pilot's certificate course at a flight school depends on the specific circumstances:

**General Rule – Vocational Flight Training:**
The context does not explicitly state whether VA can pay for a standalone private pilot certificate course at a flight school in a purely vocational context.

**College Degree Programs:**
VA *can* pay for private pilot certification if "the course is listed under 14 CFR Part 141 as an approved part of the standard college degree the student is pursuing, or is listed as in house 14 CFR Part 61 training and approved by the State Approving Agency (SAA)." [4]

Additionally, flight schools and flight training centers under contract with colleges or universities "may only provide flight training for all programs **above private pilot**" — suggesting that private pilot training in that contracted context must be handled differently (e.g., directly through the college under Part 61 or Part 141). [4]

**Key Prerequisite:**
Regardless of the program, all students "must have a second-class medical certificate prior to the approval and start of any flight training program, unless the training is conducted under FAR Part 61." [4]

---

**In summary**, the context does not clearly address VA payment for a standalone vocational private pilot course at a flight school outside of a college degree program. For that specific scenario: **"I don't know based on the provided context."**

2.02 ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA FOR VOCATIONAL FLIGHT TRAINING
retrieved_chunks.content:

[NOTE: A first-class medical certificate is valid for six months for operations requiring an airline transport pilot certificate. It is valid for twelve months for operations requiring other flight certificates. In essence a first class medical certificate is valid for six months; then it becomes a second class medical certificate for six months (14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)(1)).
 
1. If a student is enrolled in a flight course other than an Airline Transport Pilot (ATP) course, the student must hold a second-class medical certificate on the first day of training. (If that course began before October 1, 1998, the student must have held that certificate continuously during training.) A second-class medical certificate expires at the end of the last day of the twelfth month following the date of examination shown on the certificate ((14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)). (See Policy Advisory dated 10/11/05)
 
Example: A second-class medical certificate issued on April 9, 2003, is valid through April 30, 2004.
 
2. If a student is enrolled in an ATP certification course, the student must hold a first-class medical certificate on the first day of training. (If that course began before October 1, 1998, the student must have held that certificate continuously during training.) A first-class medical certificate expires at the end of the last day of the sixth month following the date of examination shown on the certificate (14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)).
 
Example: A first-class medical certificate issued on June 12, 2004, is valid through December 31, 2004.]
 
3. A student in a ground instructor course does not need to have a medical certificate.
 
4. A student must have the proper medical certificate at the start of training.
 
NOTE: Flight schools and training centers certify on VA Form 22-1999, Enrollment Certification, that the student has a private pilot's certificate and that they have copies of the required medical certificate.

retrieved_chunks.title: M22-4, Part 10, Compliance Survey. Chapter 04, Procedures Applicable to Facilities Offering Specific Types of Training.
heading_path: M22-4, Part 10, Compliance Survey. Chapter 04, Procedures Applicable to Facilities Offering Specific Types of Training.
SUBCHAPTER 03. AREAS OF REVIEW FOR FLIGHT SCHOOLS
4.35 POSSESSION OF VALID PRIVATE PILOT CERTIFICATE OR HIGHER RATING AND APPROPRIATE MEDICAL CERTIFICATE
retrieved_chunks.content:
1. A veteran, service member, or reservist who has basic eligibility to receive educational assistance allowance under the provisions of chapters 30, 32, or 1606, may receive educational assistance for flight training provided that the student also possesses an unlimited private pilot certificate issued pursuant to FAR part 61, subpart E, or higher certificate or rating before enrolling in the flight training course. Benefits are not payable if the individual possesses only a student pilot or recreational pilot certificate before entry into the approved flight training course. Recreational certificates do not meet the private pilot certificate requirement. For service members on flight status, a commercial pilot certificate (fixed wing or rotor craft) will satisfy this requirement. In order to show that the student meets this requirement, the school must maintain in its records a photocopy of the student's private pilot certificate or higher rating or certificate.

2. The student must also meet the medical requirements for the flight course being pursued. The medical requirements for the course must be met at the time of initial enrollment and continuously during pursuit of the flight training course. For all flight training courses except the ATP certificate, the student must possess a valid 2nd class medical certificate. For the ATP course, the student must possess a valid 1st class medical certificate.

3. A 1st class medical certificate is valid until the last day of the sixth month after the month of medical examination. The certificate then reverts to a 2nd class medical certificate, which is only valid for six additional months, because the expiration date is determined as stated in subparagraph (4) below.

4. A 2nd class medical certificate is valid until the last day of the 12th month after the month of medical examination.  The certificate then reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate.

EXAMPLES:

A 1st class medical certificate based upon a medical examination conducted on January 15, 1996, expires for ATP training on July 31, 1996. The certificate reverts to a 2nd class medical certificate, acceptable for all other flight training. The 2nd class medical certificate expires on January 31, 1997. The certificate reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate, valid only for private pilot operations, and not acceptable for flight training.

A 2nd class medical certificate based upon a medical examination conducted on January 15, 1996, expires on January 31, 1997. The certificate reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate, valid only for private pilot operations, and not acceptable for flight training.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
heading_path: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
2.02 ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA FOR VOCATIONAL FLIGHT TRAINING
retrieved_chunks.content:
1. [Certifications as to Medical Certificates. For the initial enrollment certification for a flight course, flight schools should put the name and date of the medical certificate in the Remarks section of the enrollment certification. If this medical certificate information is not submitted, RPOs will develop for it. If the medical certificate is not valid on the day that the course begins, RPOs will deny the claim.
 

NOTE: If the Enrollment Certification is accompanied by VA Form 22-6553c, Monthly Certification of Flight Training, the adjudicator should accept the certification shown on this form as to the student's medical certificate.

2. Questions and Answers

Question 1: If the claimant is enrolled in consecutive flight courses at the same flight school (CFI, CFII, etc.), do we need medical certificate information each time?

Answer 1: No, medical certificate information should not be requested each time a claimant is enrolled in consecutive flight courses when the claimant has a valid medical certificate covering the beginning date of each course in question.

Example: Jack received his second class medical certificate which was dated September 1, 2002. He starts a course on October 2, 2002, and completes the course on November 5, 2002, and begins a new course on November 7, 2002. Since a second class medical certificate is good for 12 months, we could pay benefits during the consecutive enrollments.

Question 2: A veteran began flight training on October 1, 2002. Although he had an expired 2nd class medical certificate, this was overlooked by him and the school certifying official. As a result, the veteran can not be paid for any training. However, if the veteran quits the course, gets the proper medical certificate and reenrolls in the course, can he began receiving benefits?

Answer 2: Yes, the veteran can begin receiving benefits if he gets the proper medical certificate and reenrolls in the course. Although we can not pay him for the portion of the course already completed, we can grant appropriate credit.]

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 9: Chapter 4 - Review of Approvals - Specific Criteria for Correspondence Schools, Flight Schools, and Job Training Establishments
heading_path: Part 9: Chapter 4 - Review of Approvals - Specific Criteria for Correspondence Schools, Flight Schools, and Job Training Establishments
SUBCHAPTER II.  SPECIFIC CRITERIA FOR FLIGHT SCHOOLS
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  A copy of the private pilot's license of each VA student.

(2)  Evidence that each VA student has completed any previous training that may be a prerequisite for the course.

(3)  A copy of the appropriate medical certificate for the course being pursued by each VA student.  Additionally, flight schools must maintain copies of all medical certificates (expired or otherwise) needed to support all periods of training at the school.

NOTE 1:  FAA regulations require only that students qualify for the appropriate medical certificates before the issuance of the objective licenses.  VA regulations require that VA students possess the appropriate medical certificates before entering training and continuously while in training.  VA students cannot receive benefits for training completed without the prescribed medical certifications.  Benefits cannot be paid retroactively.

NOTE 2:  Public Law 105-368, the Veterans Programs Enhancement Act of 1998, amended the medical certificate requirements for vocational flight training courses beginning on or after October 1, 1998.  VA students must still possess the appropriate medical certificates before entering training courses.  However, the requirement to continue to have the appropriate medical certificates while pursuing training has been eliminated.

(4)  A copy of the daily flight log for each VA student.

(5)  A copy of the permanent ground school record for each VA student.

(6)  A progress log for each VA student.

(7)  An invoice of flight charges for individual flights.

(8)  Daily flight sheets identifying records upon which the 85-15 percent ratio may be computed.

(9)  A continuous hour meter record for each aircraft.

(10)  Invoice or flight tickets signed by the student and instructor showing hour meter reading, type of aircraft and aircraft identification number.

heading_path: Part 4: Chapter 2 - Flight, Correspondence, Licensing and Certification, National Examinations, and Preparatory Courses
2.10  COMBINATION COLLEGE DEGREE – FLIGHT TRAINING PROGRAMS
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. College Degrees.

1. All students must have a  second-class medical certificate prior to the approval and start of any flight training program, unless the training is conducted under FAR Part 61.  Students do not have to take flight courses in the order specified in subparagraph 2.03a (2).

2. A student may receive benefits for private pilot certification provided the course is listed under 14 CFR Part 141 as an approved part of the standard college degree the student is pursing, or is listed as in house 14 CFR Part 61 training and approved by the State Approving Agency (SAA).

3. Flight schools and flight training centers, under contract with colleges or universities, may only provide flight training for all programs above private pilot and must be identified on their Part 141 Air Agency Certificate

4. The degree requirements must specifically identify the required number flight hours for each flight course in the school catalog. Training beyond the degree requirements is vocational.  Students under chapters 30, 32, 33 and 1606, may interrupt their college program and receive reimbursement for flight training above the private pilot level at a flight school or flight training center.  The student may return to college training without being charged with a change of program if they meet all of the following conditions:

[i.] The flight training pursued at the flight school or flight training center does not exceed the amount required for the degree.

[ii.] The college or university grants full credit for the flight course(s) in partial fulfillment of the degree requirements.

[iii.] Students continue to pursue the same degree programs.

b. Chapter 35.

Go to Top

results.answer:
## Medical Certificate Requirement for Non-ATP Flight Courses

For a flight course **other than an Airline Transport Pilot (ATP) course**, a VA student must possess a **valid second-class medical certificate** before entering training. [1][2]

### Key Details:

- **Timing:** The student must have the proper medical certificate **at the start of training** (i.e., on the first day of the course). [1]
- **Validity period:** A second-class medical certificate is valid until **the last day of the 12th month** following the date of the medical examination. [2]
  - *Example: A second-class medical certificate issued on April 9, 2003, is valid through April 30, 2004.* [1]
- **After expiration:** The certificate reverts to a **third-class medical certificate**, which is valid only for private pilot operations and is **not acceptable for VA flight training**. [2]

### Additional Notes:

- For courses beginning **on or after October 1, 1998**, the student must hold the certificate on the first day of training, but the requirement to maintain it **continuously throughout training** has been eliminated. [4]
- Flight schools must maintain **copies of all medical certificates** (expired or otherwise) needed to support all periods of training. [4]
- A third-class medical certificate or a lapsed second-class certificate does **not** satisfy the requirement. [2]

results.query: Can a Chapter 35 beneficiary receive VA benefits for vocational flight training?
results.expected_answer: No. Chapter 35 and Section 901 beneficiaries are NOT eligible for vocational flight training.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 7 - VA Education Loans PROGRAM REPEALED See CIR. 22-04-01, PARAGRAPH 9
heading_path: Part 7: Chapter 7 - VA Education Loans PROGRAM REPEALED See CIR. 22-04-01, PARAGRAPH 9
retrieved_chunks.content:
The education loan program was repealed for trainees under chapter 32, and for sons and daughters under chapter 35. 

(ii)  Basic eligibility to education loans was terminated for students under chapters 34 and 35 who were still within their delimiting       periods for educational assistance benefits, except for veterans and servicepersons under chapter 34 pursuing courses of vocational flight training at a 60 percent reimbursement rate.  (NOTE: Public Law 97-35 also repealed 38 U.S.C. 1677 and prohibited the commencement of a program of flight training after September 30, 1981. Persons who were already enrolled in a flight training program as of August 31, 1981, were permitted to continue in training provided that enrollment remained continuous.) 

(iii)  Provisions for post-delimiting date education loans to veterans under chapter 34, and to spouses and surviving spouses under chapter 35 were not amended.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 3 - Eligibility Issues
heading_path: a. DATE OF DEATH and PERMANENT AND TOTAL DISABILITY DATE
retrieved_chunks.content:
Veteran died on active duty or DEA date is within 3 years of Veteran’s release from active duty date. 

NOTE 9:  If other events are more advantageous than this 20-year generated delimiting date, leave this field blank.

d. PRIOR VA TRAINING. Beneficiaries may not receive more than 81 total months of benefits under two or more VA programs. If there is any indication that the beneficiary previously received VA education benefits, determine the amount of prior training used by reviewing BDN and the Long-Term Solution (LTS). In order to charge entitlement for prior VA training when entitlement used is given in months and fractions of a month, the fractional month must be converted to days. Use table 4 in part IV, chapter 14.

EXAMPLE 1: The beneficiary used 13.75 months of chapter 32 entitlement and is now applying for chapter 35 benefits. Convert .75 months to 22 days. Enter the prior training as 13 months and 22 days.

In addition, BDN awards initial entitlement of 45 months; however, unless the beneficiary’s first enrollment began before August 1, 2018, the beneficiary has initial entitlement of 36 months.

(1) CHAP 35 MOS DAYS. If the beneficiary has prior chapter 35 training or is to be charged excessive entitlement for Special Restorative Training (SRT), enter the number of months and days in CHAP 35 MOS and DAYS. (See par. 4.05.)

NOTE 10:  If enrollment begins on or after August 1, 2018, input 9 months and 00 days in this field so BDN will reduce initial entitlement to 36 months.  If no enrollment is in TIMS, but due to the eligibility period or retroactive payment rules, payments prior to August 1, 2018, would not be made, input 9 months and 00 days in this field so BDN will reduce entitlement to 36 months.

NOTE 11:  If enrollment begins prior to August 1, 2018, make no adjustment to this field.  If no enrollment is in TIMS, but due to the eligibility period or retroactive payment rules, payments prior to August 1, 2018, are possible, make no adjustment to this field and edit any Certificate of Eligibility to state “You have 45 months and 0 days of full-time benefits if you begin using your entitlement (attending a training program) prior to August 1, 2018; you will have 36 months of full-time benefits if you begin using your entitlement (attending a training program) on or after August 1, 2018.” 

(2) OTHER. Enter the months and days of beneficiary’s previous training under VA education programs other than chapter 35.

NOTE 12:  Do not include any Chapter 31 usage by beneficiary. 

(3) EXAMPLES.  Examples that illustrate the fields above are as follows.

EXAMPLE

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 3 - Eligibility Issues
heading_path: 3.03 ESTABLISHING QUALIFYING SERVICE-CONNECTED DISABILITY OR DEATH
retrieved_chunks.content: NOTE 2:When the Veteran has not filed a claim for disability or when the Veteran's rating is less than 100% and shows no individual unemployability, the beneficiary has not established that he or she is an "eligible person" under 38 CFR 21.3021(a) and therefore has not established "status" as a proper claimant for chapter 35 benefits. The Court of Veterans Appeals has ruled that a beneficiary must establish with a preponderance of the evidence (more than 50%) that he or she is a proper beneficiary before being entitled to the benefits and protections of Title 38. Since "status" is not established, VA does not have a duty to further develop the claim.

If there is any indication that the beneficiary previously received VA education benefits, determine the amount of prior training used by reviewing BDN and the Long-Term Solution (LTS). In order to charge entitlement for prior VA training when entitlement used is given in months and fractions of a month, the fractional month must be converted to days. Use table 4 in part IV, chapter 14.

EXAMPLE 1: The beneficiary used 13.75 months of chapter 32 entitlement and is now applying for chapter 35 benefits. Convert .75 months to 22 days. Enter the prior training as 13 months and 22 days.

In addition, BDN awards initial entitlement of 45 months; however, unless the beneficiary’s first enrollment began before August 1, 2018, the beneficiary has initial entitlement of 36 months.

(1) CHAP 35 MOS DAYS. If the beneficiary has prior chapter 35 training or is to be charged excessive entitlement for Special Restorative Training (SRT), enter the number of months and days in CHAP 35 MOS and DAYS. (See par. 4.05.)

NOTE 10:  If enrollment begins on or after August 1, 2018, input 9 months and 00 days in this field so BDN will reduce initial entitlement to 36 months.  If no enrollment is in TIMS, but due to the eligibility period or retroactive payment rules, payments prior to August 1, 2018, would not be made, input 9 months and 00 days in this field so BDN will reduce entitlement to 36 months.

NOTE 11:  If enrollment begins prior to August 1, 2018, make no adjustment to this field.  If no enrollment is in TIMS, but due to the eligibility period or retroactive payment rules, payments prior to August 1, 2018, are possible, make no adjustment to this field and edit any Certificate of Eligibility to state “You have 45 months and 0 days of full-time benefits if you begin using your entitlement (attending a training program) prior to August 1, 2018; you will have 36 months of full-time benefits if you begin using your entitlement (attending a training program) on or after August 1, 2018.” 

(2) OTHER. Enter the months and days of beneficiary’s previous training under VA education programs other than chapter 35.

NOTE 12:  Do not include any Chapter 31 usage by beneficiary. 

(3) EXAMPLES.  Examples that illustrate the fields above are as follows.

EXAMPLE

[Chunk 4] Source: 554400000073695 — Part 7: Chapter 3 - Eligibility Issues Heading: Part 7: Chapter 3 - Eligibility Issues > SUBCHAPTER I.  APPLICATIONS FOR BENEFITS > 3.03 ESTABLISHING QUALIFYING SERVICE-CONNECTED DISABILITY OR DEATH > a. General

NOTE 2:When the Veteran has not filed a claim for disability or when the Veteran's rating is less than 100% and shows no individual unemployability, the beneficiary has not established that he or she is an "eligible person" under 38 CFR 21.3021(a) and therefore has not established "status" as a proper claimant for chapter 35 benefits. The Court of Veterans Appeals has ruled that a beneficiary must establish with a preponderance of the evidence (more than 50%) that he or she is a proper beneficiary before being entitled to the benefits and protections of Title 38. Since "status" is not established, VA does not have a duty to further develop the claim.

[Chunk 5] Source: 554400000076131 — Part 3: Chapter 8 - Periodic Education Certifications Heading: Part 3: Chapter 8 - Periodic Education Certifications > 8.05  VA FORM 22-6553c, MONTHLY CERTIFICATION OF FLIGHT TRAINING

NOTE:  This section applies to chapters 30, 32, and [1606 only.  Flight training is not available for chapter 35.  Chapter 30 flight certifications are processed by the RPOs.  Chapter 32 and 1606 flight certifications are processed by the Buffalo RPO.  Currently, flight cases are processed entirely outside BDN], using manual procedures or a PC (chapter 30).
results.answer:
No. Chapter 35 beneficiaries **cannot** receive reimbursement or VA benefits for vocational flight training.

This is explicitly stated in two chunks:

- "Chapter 35 and section 901 of Public Law 96-342 trainees may **not** receive reimbursement for vocational flight training." [1]
- "Flight training is not available for chapter 35." [5]

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 6 - Measurement of College Level Courses
heading_path: Part 4: Chapter 6 - Measurement of College Level Courses
retrieved_chunks.content:
ROLE DESCRIPTION

f. Adjudicator's role

Adjudicators review the enrollment certification and, if necessary, the school's approval data and

·     determine the course measurement method that applies to the courses certified by the school.

·     determine the training time for the particular enrollment.

·     enter the appropriate Training Time code in the BDN (Benefits Delivery Network) system.

NOTE:  For instructions on completing BDN award screens, see part II, paragraphs 6.11 and 6.13 for chapters 30, 32, and 35 and part VIII, paragraphs 4.04 and 4.05 for chapter 1606.

g.  BDN's role

The BDN calculates the correct monthly rate based on the Training Time code entered on the award screen, computes the amount of payment due, and issues a payment request to the Treasury Department.

retrieved_chunks.title: 1607 Part 11: Chapter 6 - Payment and Authorization Processes
heading_path: 1607 Part 11: Chapter 6 - Payment and Authorization Processes
retrieved_chunks.content:
NOTE: BDN will not correctly calculate the rate in all cases for servicepersons at any training time and veterans at less than 1/'2 time if the individual has a chapter 1606 kicker.

When calculating the monthly rate due a student based on training time or duty status, first determine the Chapter 1607 statutory rate due and then add in the proportional kicker based on training time. If training time is less than 1/2 time but 1/4 time or more, pay 1/4 of the Chapter 1606 kicker. If BDN is not paying the rate correctly, override the rate on the 312 screen with an "M" override.

In all three examples below the term is 1/6/06 through 5/12/06 (5/13 no pay date), 1/4 time training (training time 0), 1607 is payable at the 60% rate, and the claimant is entitled to a $350.00 1606 kicker. For a student in the 60% category the maximum monthly rate payable for less than half-time training (effective 10/1/05) is $310.20.

Example 1: Tuition and fees are 50.00. The calculated monthly rate is $11.81 and one quarter of the 1606 kicker is $87.50. Currently BDN would pay only the $11.81, however the total award due the claimant is $99.31 (11.81 + 87.50). Use the M override on this award to pay $99.31.

Example 2: Tuition and fees are $1500.00. The calculated monthly rate is $354.33 to fully reimburse tuition and fees; BDN included a portion of the kicker ($44.13) to reach the rate necessary to reimburse the student fully. In reality, the total award due the claimant is $397.70 ($310.20 + 87.50). Use the M override on this award to pay $397.70.

Example 3: Tuition and fees are $5000.00. The calculated monthly rate is $1181.10 to fully reimburse tuition and fees, BDN will cap the rate at $310.20 and include the full $87.50 kicker in order to pay the maximum amount payable to the student $310.20 + $87.50). There is no need to use the M override in this scenario, we are paying the maximum amount payable.

In cases in which the student is due a lump sum award, and BDN is not paying the full amount due, override the monthly rate displayed on the 312 screen using the "M" override. Unfortunately, the student will not receive a lump sum award in this instance, but will receive the 1606 kicker included in his payment each month.

 Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: PART 01, GENERAL. CHAPTER 02, DEFINITIONS
retrieved_chunks.content:
INDIVIDUAL READY RESERVE (IRR) An individual who receives no pay and is not obligated to drill, conduct annual training, or participate in any military activities until activated by the Presidential Reserve Call-Up Authority.


INTERVAL PAYMENT Educational assistance benefits paid during the intervals between terms at a school or between terms when transferring from one school to another while the student is in the same program. Persons on active duty or training at the less than one-half time rate in the preceding term are not entitled to interval payments. The payment of intervals has been eliminated as of August 1, 2011.


INTERACTIVE VOICE RESPONSE (IVR) Claimants who must submit monthly verifications of their enrollment may call a toll free telephone number which uses IVR technology to report their attendance.


Go To Top


JOURNEYMAN Someone who completed an apprenticeship program and is fully trained in a trade or craft, but who is not yet a master.


Go To Top


KICKERS Amounts contributed by DOD to an education fund on behalf of participants to encourage enlistment or retention in the Armed Forces. The military services may use different terms such as "Army College Fund" or "Navy College Fund", etc. These amounts are added to any other education benefits to which the person may be entitled.


Go To Top


LACAS (LICENSING AND CERTIFICATION APPROVAL SYSTEM) An Internet based database application for licensing and certification approvals.


LUMP SUM PAYMENT An amount of educational assistance paid for the entire term, quarter, or semester.


Go To Top


MATRICULATED STUDENT A college or university student who has satisfied all prerequisites for formal admission and recognition by the institution as a degree-seeking student. This does not mean that an undergraduate student must have formally been accepted into a specific major field of study before he or she may be classified as "matriculated."

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 8 - Interval Payment
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  Intervals between terms at the same school which span a full calendar month or more.

EXAMPLE:  The interval from December 19, 1990, to February 2, 1991, spans the full calendar month of January.  Therefore, do not pay for this interval. 

(2)  Intervals between terms which exceed 30 days but are less than a full calendar month if the student transfers from one school to another without a change of program.

(3)  Intervals between terms, regardless of length, when the student transfers from one school to another and also changes programs.

NOTE:  If the student remains in the same school but changes programs, authorize payment for the interval if otherwise appropriate.

(4)  Intervals between terms, regardless of length, if the student is: 

(a)  On active duty, or

(b)  Training at a less-than-half time rate on the last day of the term preceding the interval;

NOTE:  The restriction in subparagraph (4)(b) above applies even if a reduction in course load during the last month did not actually result in a reduction in benefits.

(5)  Intervals which occur between school years at a school which is not organized on a term, quarter or semester basis.

(6)  Intervals between terms for which a student requests, before award authorization, that no benefits be paid.

(7)  (See RPO Letter 22-07-01) Intervals in which the student will exhaust his or her entitlement and it is to the student's advantage not to receive benefits for the interval to conserve entitlement.  (Use FL 22-893, Non-Payment of Benefits During Intervals Between Terms and Repurchase of Entitlement, to explain to the student that benefits have not been authorized for the interval to conserve enough entitlement to allow re enrollment in a subsequent term.)

(8)  Intervals between terms when the student withdraws from all courses in the term preceding the interval or discontinues training before the scheduled start of an interval in a school not organized on a term, quarter or semester basis.

(9)  Intervals which are described in paragraphs 8.02c and 8.03. 

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.6 - Reimbursements, Fees, and Overpayments
heading_path: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.6 - Reimbursements, Fees, and Overpayments
retrieved_chunks.content:
Effective August 1, 2011, eligibility for interval pay (break pay) has been restricted to periods where a school is closed due to an Executive Order of the President or an emergency situation (such as a natural disaster or strike). These payments are limited to 4 weeks in any 12-month period.
 

Sources:

Part 12, Chapter 33. Chapter 12 - Public Laws and Regulations.Training Slides PL111-377, Post-9/11 Veterans Educational Assistance Improvements Act of 2010, Title II Other Educational Assistance Matters, SEC. 206. Modification of Authority to Make Certain Interval Payments
 

Go To Top

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 8 - Interval Payment
heading_path: 8.07  ENTITLEMENT CHARGE FOR INTERVALS BETWEEN TERMS WITH NON CREDIT COURSES
retrieved_chunks.content:
Go to Top

If an eligible person under chapter 35 is enrolled in entitlement free courses (i.e., non credit deficiency, remedial, or refresher courses or high school courses) and interval payment is payable, be sure to charge the proper entitlement for the interval.  Ordinarily, the entitlement charge for the interval is the same as for the preceding term.  However, if the eligible person's 5 months of entitlement free training expires during the interval, begin charging full entitlement on the day after the entitlement free training expires.  (See Part 7: Chapter 1 - General for a full explanation of entitlement free training under ch. 35.)

Go to Top

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, interval payments are no longer payable for GI Bill training effective **August 1, 2011**, as established by SEC 206 PL 111-377, 38 U.S.C. 3680. [1][2]

The only exception noted is for Chapter 33 (Post-9/11 GI Bill), where interval pay (break pay) may still be authorized when a school is closed due to an Executive Order of the President or an emergency situation (such as a natural disaster or strike), limited to **4 weeks in any 12-month period**. [4]

results.query: When is a summer-term interval payable even if it exceeds 30 days?
results.expected_answer: When the summer term is at least 8 weeks long

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 8 - Interval Payment
retrieved_chunks.content:
Explanation:  The student is eligible for payment for the spring-summer interval since that interval is less than 30 days although the summer term, which consisted of two overlapping sessions, is less than 8 weeks.

NOTE:  Although the overlapping days are not counted twice, the combination of the two 3/4 training times constitutes full-time training from July 6, 1991, to July 10, 1991.

The student is not eligible for payment for the summer-fall interval since that interval is more than 30 days, although less than a full calendar month, and the summer term is less than 8 weeks.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 8 - Interval Payment
retrieved_chunks.content:
Explanation:  The student is eligible for payment for both the spring-summer and summer-fall intervals since the summer term is at least 8 weeks long and both intervals are less than a full calendar month.  (See subpar. d below on the method for computing the length of a summer term.)  Authorize payment for the interval between the summer sessions since the interval does not exceed 30 days.

EXAMPLE 2:  A student is enrolled 3/4 time at the same school as follows:

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, a summer-term interval that exceeds 30 days is **not payable**. The rules work as follows:

- A summer-term interval is payable only if it is **less than a full calendar month** (and the summer term is at least 8 weeks long) [2], **or** if it is **less than 30 days** (even when the summer term is less than 8 weeks) [1].

Specifically, the context states that a student **is not eligible** for payment for a summer-fall interval that is "more than 30 days, although less than a full calendar month, and the summer term is less than 8 weeks." [1]

In other words, there is no scenario described in the provided context where a summer-term interval exceeding 30 days is payable. The two conditions for payability are:

1. The interval is **less than 30 days** (regardless of summer term length), **or**
2. The interval is **less than a full calendar month** AND the summer term is **at least 8 weeks long** [1][2].

If neither condition is met, the interval is not payable.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 5 - Notice of Exception Processing
heading_path: Part 7: Chapter 5 - Notice of Exception Processing
retrieved_chunks.content:
See part II, chapter 8, for an overview of NOE (Notice of Exception) processing.  Most of the messages are generated on BDN (Benefits Delivery Network) using the NOES (Notice of Exception System) command.  However, some of the messages are still printed on VA Form 20-8270a, Education Master Record - Audit Writeout, or VA Form 20-6560, Notice of Benefit Payment Transaction.  The messages are issued to advise field stations of the errors or exceptions found in the edit of the transaction processed or of an inconsistency resulting from processing the transaction against the master record.  A review of the content of the message and the record will indicate whether the transaction has been rejected and resubmission is required.  Some transactions are submitted together but are processed separately.  If BDN split (separated) the transaction into two or more separate transactions, the field station is only required to correct and resubmit the rejected transaction(s).

NOTE 1:  In most instances, these messages could be issued for chapter 34 records as well as for chapter 35.  However, most of the chapter 34 messages have been deleted.  The term "claimant's folder" refers to both a veteran's claim folder and a claimant's chapter 35 DEA (Dependents' Educational Assistance) folder.  MM-DD-YY refers to the way that the date information is submitted with MM being the two digits of the month, DD being the two digits of the day, and YY being the two digits of the year.

NOTE 2:  Corrections (02V transactions) can be made either directly on a BDN screen by using the CORR (Correction) command or by using the Alternate Means of Input.  See part II, paragraph 8.13, for BDN entries.  See paragraph 2.06 for Alternate Means of Input entries.  For additional information concerning 02V transactions, see MP-6, part IV, supplement number 6.1.

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 9 - Certificates of Eligibility and Disallowances
heading_path: Part 3: Chapter 9 - Certificates of Eligibility and Disallowances
SUBCHAPTER I.  ISSUING CERTIFICATES OF ELIGIBILITY
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  Chapter 32 and Section 903.  File a print of a completed Chapter 32 Eligibility (310) screen on the left side of the claims [or education] folder.

(2)  Chapter 35.  File a print of a completed Chapter 35 Eligibility (311) screen [ ] on the left side of the Dependent Education Assistance (DEA) folder.  Send a copy of the documentation to the claims folder.

(3)  Chapter 1606.  File a print of the DOD screen in the center of the claims [or the left side of the education] folder.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1) Electronic Folder Management System. All Education processing is done within TIMS. This is the primary folder for storing claims related information. For additional information regarding creating folders in TIMS, refer to Chapter 14, Upstream Procedures.

NOTE: The only types of folders that can be recorded in BDN (Benefit Delivery Network) are claims folders, Chapter 30 folders, DEA (Dependents’ Educational Assistance) folders, and EDU (Education) folders. BDN does not indicate whether a TIMS folder or a paper folder exists.

(2) Claims Folder. This is a three-part red paper folder known as red-rope. It has three flaps with fasteners attached on the inside. Claims folders were used to process Chapter 32, Section 903, and Chapter 1606 benefits before June 1994. Since June 1994, Education (EDU) folders were used for these benefits. The Waco RO used paper claims folders for Section 901 claims until the Muskogee RPO assumed jurisdiction for this benefit in 2005. Since then, Section 901 records have been converted to TIMS. The Chapter 34 benefit of Title 38 (which expired on December 31, 1989) was processed entirely with claims folders.

(3) CH30 (Chapter 30) Folder. This is a two-part paper folder containing Chapter 30 material only. Initially, RPOs built two-part gray folders for Chapter 30 claims until the stock of those folders was depleted. Then the RPOs used Kraft folders (light brown in color) as Chapter 30 folders. These folders were discontinued in the 1990s and replaced with EDU (Education) folders.

(4) EDU Folder. This is a two-part Kraft brown paper folder containing material for Chapters 30, 32, 1606, or Section 903. The physical EDU folders have not been created since the 1990s. However, the TIMS folders for all education benefits (except Chapter 35) are recorded as EDU folders in BDN.

NOTE 1: For claims established before October 16, 2000, a Veteran could have two folders (both an EDU and a CH30 folder) shown in BDN and in some instances two paper folders. In this situation, RPOs had two options: to retain both folders, or to destroy one folder and retain only one folder. If the RPO decided to destroy a folder, the RPO needed to request and adjustment in BDN from the Records Management Center (RMC). A claimant should only have one electronic folder.

NOTE 2: The multiple folders in BIRLS (Benefits Information Record Location System) or BDN occurred because before October 1, 2000, the CEST (Claims Establishment) processing for Chapter 30 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of a CH30 folder and the CEST processing for Chapters 32 and 1606 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of an EDU folder. CEST processing was changed effective October 1, 2000, to automatically create only an EDU folder in BIRLS for these education benefits.

(5) Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) Folder. This is a two-part blue paper folder created for Chapter 35 benefits. These paper folders are seldom used except in rare situations when a dependent has no SSN (Social Security Number), there is a second (subsequent) spouse, or a 10th (subsequent) child and TIMS is unable to support the paperless folder.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER I.  HINES AND BIRLS ISSUES
b.  Claims Establishment Actions to Update BIRLS on Supplemental DEA Claims
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary already has a Hines master record but does not have a BIRLS BID record (or a DEA folder in the BID record), process as in paragraph a, except during the CEST action, also enter "VID" (Veteran Identification Data) in the SCREEN NUMBER field at the top of the Ready Screen.  (The entry of "VID" keys the system to access BIRLS.)

(2)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary already has a Hines master record and has a BIRLS BID record with a DEA folder in the BID record, but the BIRLS information for the individual is incomplete or wrong in the BID record, use the BUPD (BIRLS Update) command to update the BIRLS information in the BID record.  Enter the correct information; then enter "UPD" in the NEXT SCREEN field.

(3)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary does not already have a Hines master record but has a BIRLS BID record with a DEA folder in the BID record, use the CEST command to process an award and establish the Hines master record.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER I.  HINES AND BIRLS ISSUES
a.  Claims Establishment Actions to Update BIRLS on Original DEA Claims.
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  Complete the Ready Screen.  Enter the command CEST, the operator's password, the veteran's file number, the payee number, the end product code, the benefit (CH35), the veteran's stub name, and the claimant's full name.  Press the Enter key.

(2)  BIRLS will show the VID (Veterans Identification Data) screen.  Enter any appropriate information for the veteran.  Then enter   "UPD" (Update) in the NEXT SCREEN field (located at the bottom right hand side of the displayed VID screen).

(3)  When CEST is for the first beneficiary, BIRLS shows the BID screen for that payee.  Complete the BID screen as follows:

(i)  Enter the beneficiary's date of birth, sex, and social security number.

(ii)  Find the field "ESTABLISH A _________ FOLDER" located toward the bottom of the screen.  Enter "Y" in the field immediately to the left of the word "ESTABLISH."  Enter "DEA" in the blank located between "ESTABLISH A" and "FOLDER."

(iii)  Enter "UPD" (to update the BID record for the payee) in the NEXT SCREEN field.

(iv)  Press the Enter key.

(4)  When CEST is for the second or subsequent payees, BIRLS shows the BID screen for the first payee if only one payee has already been established or the NUM (Number) screen if two or more payees have already been established.  In either case, enter ADD

[Chunk 1] Source: 554400000073754 — Part 7: Chapter 5 - Notice of Exception Processing Heading: Part 7: Chapter 5 - Notice of Exception Processing > 5.01  GENERAL
See part II, chapter 8, for an overview of NOE (Notice of Exception) processing.  Most of the messages are generated on BDN (Benefits Delivery Network) using the NOES (Notice of Exception System) command.  However, some of the messages are still printed on VA Form 20-8270a, Education Master Record - Audit Writeout, or VA Form 20-6560, Notice of Benefit Payment Transaction.  The messages are issued to advise field stations of the errors or exceptions found in the edit of the transaction processed or of an inconsistency resulting from processing the transaction against the master record.  A review of the content of the message and the record will indicate whether the transaction has been rejected and resubmission is required.  Some transactions are submitted together but are processed separately.  If BDN split (separated) the transaction into two or more separate transactions, the field station is only required to correct and resubmit the rejected transaction(s).

NOTE 1:  In most instances, these messages could be issued for chapter 34 records as well as for chapter 35.  However, most of the chapter 34 messages have been deleted.  The term "claimant's folder" refers to both a veteran's claim folder and a claimant's chapter 35 DEA (Dependents' Educational Assistance) folder.  MM-DD-YY refers to the way that the date information is submitted with MM being the two digits of the month, DD being the two digits of the day, and YY being the two digits of the year.

NOTE 2:  Corrections (02V transactions) can be made either directly on a BDN screen by using the CORR (Correction) command or by using the Alternate Means of Input.  See part II, paragraph 8.13, for BDN entries.  See paragraph 2.06 for Alternate Means of Input entries.  For additional information concerning 02V transactions, see MP-6, part IV, supplement number 6.1.

Go to Top

[Chunk 2] Source: 554400000076203 — Part 3: Chapter 9 - Certificates of Eligibility and Disallowances Heading: Part 3: Chapter 9 - Certificates of Eligibility and Disallowances > SUBCHAPTER I.  ISSUING CERTIFICATES OF ELIGIBILITY > 9.03  SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION AND AUTHORIZATION > a.

(1)  Chapter 32 and Section 903.  File a print of a completed Chapter 32 Eligibility (310) screen on the left side of the claims [or education] folder.

(2)  Chapter 35.  File a print of a completed Chapter 35 Eligibility (311) screen [ ] on the left side of the Dependent Education Assistance (DEA) folder.  Send a copy of the documentation to the claims folder.

(3)  Chapter 1606.  File a print of the DOD screen in the center of the claims [or the left side of the education] folder.

[Chunk 3] Source: 554400000073486 — Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues Heading: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues > SUBCHAPTER I.  JURISDICTION PROCEDURES > 1.01  GENERAL > c. Education Folders.

(1) Electronic Folder Management System. All Education processing is done within TIMS. This is the primary folder for storing claims related information. For additional information regarding creating folders in TIMS, refer to Chapter 14, Upstream Procedures.

NOTE: The only types of folders that can be recorded in BDN (Benefit Delivery Network) are claims folders, Chapter 30 folders, DEA (Dependents’ Educational Assistance) folders, and EDU (Education) folders. BDN does not indicate whether a TIMS folder or a paper folder exists.

(2) Claims Folder. This is a three-part red paper folder known as red-rope. It has three flaps with fasteners attached on the inside. Claims folders were used to process Chapter 32, Section 903, and Chapter 1606 benefits before June 1994. Since June 1994, Education (EDU) folders were used for these benefits. The Waco RO used paper claims folders for Section 901 claims until the Muskogee RPO assumed jurisdiction for this benefit in 2005. Since then, Section 901 records have been converted to TIMS. The Chapter 34 benefit of Title 38 (which expired on December 31, 1989) was processed entirely with claims folders.

(3) CH30 (Chapter 30) Folder. This is a two-part paper folder containing Chapter 30 material only. Initially, RPOs built two-part gray folders for Chapter 30 claims until the stock of those folders was depleted. Then the RPOs used Kraft folders (light brown in color) as Chapter 30 folders. These folders were discontinued in the 1990s and replaced with EDU (Education) folders.

(4) EDU Folder. This is a two-part Kraft brown paper folder containing material for Chapters 30, 32, 1606, or Section 903. The physical EDU folders have not been created since the 1990s. However, the TIMS folders for all education benefits (except Chapter 35) are recorded as EDU folders in BDN.

NOTE 1: For claims established before October 16, 2000, a Veteran could have two folders (both an EDU and a CH30 folder) shown in BDN and in some instances two paper folders. In this situation, RPOs had two options: to retain both folders, or to destroy one folder and retain only one folder. If the RPO decided to destroy a folder, the RPO needed to request and adjustment in BDN from the Records Management Center (RMC). A claimant should only have one electronic folder.

NOTE 2: The multiple folders in BIRLS (Benefits Information Record Location System) or BDN occurred because before October 1, 2000, the CEST (Claims Establishment) processing for Chapter 30 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of a CH30 folder and the CEST processing for Chapters 32 and 1606 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of an EDU folder. CEST processing was changed effective October 1, 2000, to automatically create only an EDU folder in BIRLS for these education benefits.

(5) Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) Folder. This is a two-part blue paper folder created for Chapter 35 benefits. These paper folders are seldom used except in rare situations when a dependent has no SSN (Social Security Number), there is a second (subsequent) spouse, or a 10th (subsequent) child and TIMS is unable to support the paperless folder.

[Chunk 4] Source: 554400000073806 — Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous Heading: Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous > SUBCHAPTER I.  HINES AND BIRLS ISSUES > 8.03  DEA CLAIMS ESTABLISHMENT PROCESSING > b.  Claims Establishment Actions to Update BIRLS on Supplemental DEA Claims

(1)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary already has a Hines master record but does not have a BIRLS BID record (or a DEA folder in the BID record), process as in paragraph a, except during the CEST action, also enter "VID" (Veteran Identification Data) in the SCREEN NUMBER field at the top of the Ready Screen.  (The entry of "VID" keys the system to access BIRLS.)

(2)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary already has a Hines master record and has a BIRLS BID record with a DEA folder in the BID record, but the BIRLS information for the individual is incomplete or wrong in the BID record, use the BUPD (BIRLS Update) command to update the BIRLS information in the BID record.  Enter the correct information; then enter "UPD" in the NEXT SCREEN field.

(3)  If the chapter 35 beneficiary does not already have a Hines master record but has a BIRLS BID record with a DEA folder in the BID record, use the CEST command to process an award and establish the Hines master record.

[Chunk 5] Source: 554400000073806 — Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous Heading: Part 7: Chapter 8 - Miscellaneous > SUBCHAPTER I.  HINES AND BIRLS ISSUES > 8.03  DEA CLAIMS ESTABLISHMENT PROCESSING > a.  Claims Establishment Actions to Update BIRLS on Original DEA Claims.

(1)  Complete the Ready Screen.  Enter the command CEST, the operator's password, the veteran's file number, the payee number, the end product code, the benefit (CH35), the veteran's stub name, and the claimant's full name.  Press the Enter key.

(2)  BIRLS will show the VID (Veterans Identification Data) screen.  Enter any appropriate information for the veteran.  Then enter   "UPD" (Update) in the NEXT SCREEN field (located at the bottom right hand side of the displayed VID screen).

(3)  When CEST is for the first beneficiary, BIRLS shows the BID screen for that payee.  Complete the BID screen as follows:

(i)  Enter the beneficiary's date of birth, sex, and social security number.

(ii)  Find the field "ESTABLISH A _________ FOLDER" located toward the bottom of the screen.  Enter "Y" in the field immediately to the left of the word "ESTABLISH."  Enter "DEA" in the blank located between "ESTABLISH A" and "FOLDER."

(iii)  Enter "UPD" (to update the BID record for the payee) in the NEXT SCREEN field.

(iv)  Press the Enter key.

(4)  When CEST is for the second or subsequent payees, BIRLS shows the BID screen for the first payee if only one payee has already been established or the NUM (Number) screen if two or more payees have already been established.  In either case, enter ADD
results.answer:
Based on the provided context, Chapter 35 DEA (Dependents' Educational Assistance) claims use a **two-part blue paper folder** called a **DEA (Dependents' Educational Assistance) Folder**. [3]

However, these paper folders are seldom used in current practice. As noted in the context, they are only used "in rare situations when a dependent has no SSN (Social Security Number), there is a second (subsequent) spouse, or a 10th (subsequent) child and TIMS is unable to support the paperless folder." [3]

For most Chapter 35 processing today, **TIMS (the Electronic Folder Management System)** serves as the primary folder for storing claims-related information. [3]

results.query: What is the automatic suspense date for EPC 270 (Notice of Disagreement)?
results.expected_answer: 60 days (all other EPCs have a 30-day suspense date)

retrieved_chunks.title: CHAPTER 6. WORK FLOW MANAGEMENT
retrieved_chunks.content:
TRANSACTIONS OVER 10 DAYS. The number of cases with uncorrected rejects which have prevented processing.

b.  PENDING AUTHORIZATION OVER 5 DAYS. The cases where all work action has been completed except for the decision to approve or disapprove the action by the authorizer. Supervisors should monitor these cases closely to insure that the number is kept to a minimum.

c.  PENDING ACCEPTANCE OVER 5 DAYS. A category not based on activities within the regional office. Cases are identified in this category because of overlaps in processing cycles or delays in posting completed transaction at the RDPC.

d. NUMBER OF REJECTS. The number of outstanding rejects is available for all education EPC Types (EDU, C30).

e. For each pending issue, the W-40 detail prints show file number, payee, EPC, benefit, folder location and date, suspense date, and days in the status category. To obtain a detail print, see Paragraph 6.02(c).


Go To Top

6.08  MATURING PENDING ISSUES - W50 SCREEN

There are two purposes for the Maturing Pending Issues screen. Column A identifies issues on which action is due, to manage the workload and assure timely processing of claims. Columns B through G identify issues on which action is past due, to identify problem areas and help resolve out-of-line situations in particular end products. The time categories for issues on the W50 screen are based on the suspense date, not the date of claim. This suspense date is automatically generated during CEST (Claims ESTablishment) and CADJ processing to establish a new pending issue, or when a future diary issue matures. For education end products, the automatic suspense date is 30 days in the future. However, for authorization issues, the generated date may be modified by the manual entry of a desired suspense date on the claims establishment screen during CEST, CADJ, or PCHG (Pending issues CHanGe) processing, or on the S99 screen when accessed by the CDEV, CDAT, or CADJ commands. For ESU issues, this can only be done by canceling the issue using WCAN, and re-establishing it using WEST (Work ESTablishment) with the new date. See Figure 6.7 for a representation of the W50 screen layout.

Figure 6.7.  Maturing Pending Issues - W50 Screen Layout

W50                                              MATURING PENDING ISSUES                                                  MM-DD-YY

retrieved_chunks.title: CHAPTER 5. QUANTITATIVE MEASUREMENT
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Incomplete Claims. Record responses to incomplete claims, letters of protest not constituting valid notices of disagreement, and other correspondence under EPC 400.

b. Future Diary Controls. Future diary controls will create an independent issue when they “mature,” that is, reach their suspense date. (See par. 6.09.) Before that date, if the future diary is no longer needed, cancel it and do not claim separate work credit. Depending on the reason for cancellation, it may be considered part of another EPC, or recorded as EPC 330.

c. Multiple Issues. Also see subparagraph d below for dependency issues.

1. Generally, only one EPC is proper even though the claim may involve multiple issues and/or award actions. When several issues are worked at the same time, claim only one EPC although the issues involved could have resulted in separate EPCs had they been received and/or worked at different times. However, when factual circumstances are unrelated, and one adjustment creates an overpayment while another adjustment increases benefits, do not simultaneously process the award adjustments. This ensures that a beneficiary retains the right to dispute a debt, or seek waiver of the recovery of the debt. (For further instructions on determining when factual circumstances are unrelated, see M22-4, pt. IV, par. 13.21.)

Example 1: An enrollment certification and a notice of reduction are received separately, but worked at the same time. Use only one EPC.

Example 2: A notice of an earlier beginning date and a notice of increase are received separately, but worked at the same time. Use only one EPC, even if two award actions are required.

Example 3: A college's drop period begins on August 26 and ends on September 15. A veteran drops a 3-credit course on August 28. He adds a 3-credit course on September 5. Both training time changes were made during the period designated for unrestricted enrollment changes. Consider them as resulting from related factual circumstances, unless there is evidence of record showing that the circumstances causing them are unrelated. Process a simultaneous award adjustment. Use only one EPC.

2. Do not record the EPC until all issues raised by the claim have been finally resolved.

Example:  An enrollment certification and a notice of reduction are received separately, but worked at the same time. Record the EPC when all issues, including mitigating circumstances if applicable, have been finally resolved.

3. When the same claim involves more than one benefit, use a separate EPC for each benefit. Take the appropriate EPC when the

retrieved_chunks.title: CHAPTER 6. WORK FLOW MANAGEMENT
retrieved_chunks.content:
a suspense date, as well as pending issues originally established as future diaries, but which have passed their diary suspense date and have become regular pending issues with a date of claim and a new suspense date. (See Section 6.09 below.) For ESU, these include pending issues established with a date of claim and a suspense date, and future diaries nearing their diary suspense date, since there is no W60 screen for ESU issues. Obtain a detail listing of cases from Column A weekly to assure timely processing of issues.

b. Columns B through G display issues which have already matured and fall in the time categories listed. The statistical information shown on the screen progresses from Column A to Column G as the individual issues mature and become past due. Supervisors should obtain detail listings from these columns at their discretion to identify and resolve problems with particular end products or organizational units.

c. For each pending issue, the W-50 detail prints show file number, payee, stub name, EPC, benefit, date of claim, suspense date, and folder location. To obtain a detail print, see Paragraph 6.02(c).


Go To Top

6.09  MATURING DIARY ISSUES - W60 SCREEN

The Maturing Diary Issue screen W60 also has two purposes. Column A identifies diaries which are reaching their suspense date and will now require action. Columns B through F and the TOTAL ISSUES column display the number of issues that have been diaried in the BDN system for future action or establishment of an actual pending issue. This information enables management to plan in advance for this segment of the workload. This screen shows only authorization issues, not ESU issues. Pending ESU future diaries which are not yet approaching their suspense dates are accessible only by the WINQ (Work INQuiry) command. See Section 6.08 for maturing ESU future diaries. See Figure 6.8 for a representation of the W60 screen layout.

Figure 6.8.  Maturing Diary Issues - W60 Screen Layout

Go To Top

6.09  MATURING DIARY ISSUES - W60 SCREEN

The Maturing Diary Issue screen W60 also has two purposes. Column A identifies diaries which are reaching their suspense date and will now require action. Columns B through F and the TOTAL ISSUES column display the number of issues that have been diaried in the BDN system for future action or establishment of an actual pending issue. This information enables management to plan in advance for this segment of the workload. This screen shows only authorization issues, not ESU issues. Pending ESU future diaries which are not yet approaching their suspense dates are accessible only by the WINQ (Work INQuiry) command. See Section 6.08 for maturing ESU future diaries. See Figure 6.8 for a representation of the W60 screen layout.

Figure 6.8.  Maturing Diary Issues - W60 Screen Layout

W60                                                      MATURING DIARY ISSUES                                             MM-DD-YY

                                                            XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

                                                     (A)              (B)                (C)               (D)              (E)                       (F)

                        TOTAL              00-07

results.query: How many years of service is excluded for a ROTC-commissioned officer under Chapter 33?
results.expected_answer: 4 years

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 5 Eligibility Excluded Service
retrieved_chunks.content:
c. Determining excluded period dates

Because chapter 33 is a title 38 benefit, the VA is responsible for prescription of regulations which govern administration of the benefit. Accordingly, VA has determined a commission under the authority of 2107(b) of Title 10 will incur a four-year exclusion period under chapter 33. The VIS application currently reports exclusion dates based on data reported by DoD, including the accession source code, in the “Excluded Obligated Periods of Service” table. The dates which appear in this table are derived data and not authoritative. The reported dates may be used as a guide for determining the exclusion period, but may contain clearly erroneous data. While the VA must rely on DoD data to make these determinations, the DoD does not determine applicability of an exclusion, nor the dates of an exclusion. VA is the sole authority for determining the applicable dates for the exclusion period, based on facts found.

Determine the exclusion period when 2107(b) is an issue by using the following process:

1. Commissioned as an officer in regular component – The exclusion period for a solider commissioned as an officer in a regular component begins on the entrance on duty date of the service period which began due to commission and ends after four years. Service begins accruing towards eligibility on the four-year anniversary of commission.

A solider who is member of a Guard or Reserve component while in the ROTC program, and who subsequently receives a commission as an officer in a regular component and has a period of delayed entry between their commission date and entrance on duty in the regular component, should have their exclusion period begin on the entrance on duty date in the regular component and end after four years. Service begins accruing towards eligibility on the four-year anniversary of their entrance on duty.

Example 1: A solider receives a commission under 2107(b) to serve as an officer in a regular component on May 23, 2019. They were a member of a Reserve component from September 27, 2017, to May 22, 2019, while participating in the ROTC program. They entered the regular component on May 23, 2019. The exclusion period is May 23, 2019, to May 22, 2023. Service begins accruing on May 23, 2019.

Example 2: A solider receives a commission under 2107(b) to serve as an officer in a regular component on May 23, 2019. They were a member of a Reserve component from September 27, 2017, to September 20, 2019, while participating in the ROTC program. They entered the regular component on September 21, 2019, after a period of delayed entry. The exclusion period is September 21, 2019, to September 20, 2023. Service begins accruing on September 21, 2023.

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 5 Eligibility Excluded Service
heading_path: 5.02 Periods of Active Duty Excluded from Eligibility Determinations
retrieved_chunks.content:
Scenario

Example ROTC:  A veteran is discharged from service with a R.O.T.C. obligated period. That period of R.O.T.C. must be excluded from his/her chapter 33 benefits eligibility. The record reflects service on active duty as follows:

September 1, 2004 to September 2, 2009. Since four years of service must be excluded. The processor will enter the service dates from September 1, 2004, to September 2, 2009, and then enter September 1, 2004, to August 31, 2008, as excluded service. Eligibility does not begin until the day after their four-year commitment has been completed. The eligible period of service for chapter 33 benefits would be September 1, 2008, to September 2, 2009.

Example Service Academy:

A West Point graduate, graduates on May 27, 2003, was commissioned on May 27, 2003, and served through May 26, 2008. The individual's academy commitment ended on May 27, 2008. The five year excluded period runs from May 27, 2003 through May 26, 2008. The first date eligibility for a period of qualifying service may be counted is May 27, 2008.

Title 10 USC 4348(a)(2)(B) for USMA, Title 10 USC 6959(a)(2)(B) for United States Naval Academy,  and Title 1010 USC 9348(a)(2)(B) for United States Air Force Academy.“will serve on active duty for at least five years immediately after such appointment”.

 Back to top
